title: Dundee cake
subtitle: Scottish fruit cake with whole almonds on top
excerpt: A light Scottish fruit cake studded with sultanas, raisins, and mixed peel, decorated with concentric rings of whole blanched almonds and baked low and slow. Keeps for a week and improves with it. Makes one 20 cm cake.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): Dundee cake documented as a Scottish fruit cake lighter than a Christmas cake, made with butter rather than lard and characterised by the whole almonds arranged on top. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: Scottish fruit cakes with currants, sultanas, and almonds appear in the household baking entries. The almond decoration is the defining feature recorded across Scottish household manuals of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze whole or in slices. Wrap tightly. Keeps for 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake improves for up to a week after baking. Wrap in baking paper and store in a tin.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 160°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Beating softened butter and sugar together until the mixture is pale and noticeably fluffy. The sugar crystals cut into the fat, forcing in air that produces a network of tiny pockets. Those pockets expand in the oven and contribute to a lighter texture. The process takes 4–5 minutes by hand or 3 minutes with a mixer; undercreaming produces a flat, dense cake.

content.text: Dundee cake is the lighter Scottish fruit cake: no treacle, no dark muscovado, no spice beyond a little mixed spice if you like it. The fruit should be plump and not overwhelm the batter. The blanched almonds on top are arranged in neat concentric rings before the cake goes into the oven; they toast as the cake bakes.
Bake low and slow. A Dundee cake that rushes burns on the outside before the centre is done. If the top is colouring too fast after an hour, lay a sheet of baking paper loosely over the tin.

the softened {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy, about 4 minutes. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{mixed-spice}} if using. Stir in the {{sultanas}}, {{raisins}}, {{glace-cherries}}, {{mixed-peel}}, {{orange}} zest, and {{brandy}} if using. The batter will be stiff.

Preheat the oven to 150°C fan. Grease and double-line a 20 cm round tin with baking paper. Spoon the batter into the tin and smooth the surface. Arrange the {{blanched-almonds}} in concentric rings on top, pressing them gently into the batter. Bake for 80–90 minutes, until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean and the almonds are deep gold. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then turn out onto a rack.
